title: 👑 Gauntlet C1 Elite ⭐⭐⭐⭐: The Final Trial

questions.prompt: Grammar — Inversion: "Under no circumstances ___ leave the building during the alarm."

options.text: students should
should students
students must
must students not

questions.explanation: Under no circumstances is a negative restrictive phrase that forces subject-auxiliary inversion: Under no circumstances should students leave…. Note: the negation is already in the fronted phrase, so mustn't or not is not added again. (a) has no inversion. (c) uses must with no inversion. (d) wrongly adds not, creating a double negative (Under no circumstances must students not leave = they must leave!).

questions.prompt: Grammar — Cleft sentence: "Rewrite — 'The deadline caused the most stress.' — as a correct it-cleft."

options.text: It was the deadline who caused the most stress.
It was the deadline that caused the most stress.
It was the deadline which it caused the most stress.
It was the deadline what caused the most stress.

questions.explanation: An it-cleft highlighting a thing uses that (or which): It was the deadline that caused the most stress. (a) uses who — only for people. (c) adds a redundant it after which, making a grammatical error. (d) uses what — what is used to open wh-clefts (What caused stress was the deadline), not as a relative in an it-cleft.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Modal of past deduction: "The exam paper was left on the desk all night. Anyone ___ the answers."

options.text: could have seen
must see
should have seen
can't have seen

questions.explanation: Anyone could have seen (past ability/possibility) — the exposure made it possible for someone to see the answers. "must see" is a present obligation (no past reference). "should have seen" implies someone was expected or obliged to see the answers. "can't have seen" expresses certainty that nobody saw — but the paper being left out makes seeing possible, not impossible.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Participle clause: "___ the task was beyond its resources, the company withdrew its bid."

options.text: Realising
Realise
Having been realised
It realised

questions.explanation: The company realised (active, simultaneous with withdrawing), so an active -ing participle is correct: Realising the task was beyond its resources, the company withdrew. The subject is the same in both clauses (the company). "Realise" is the base form (no tense). "Having been realised" is a passive past participle — the task can't realise itself. "It realised" is a full clause (would need a conjunction).

questions.prompt: Vocabulary — Nominalisation: "Choose the most formal academic rephrasing of 'The scientists achieved a breakthrough.'"

options.text: The scientists' achievement of a breakthrough marked a turning point.
The scientists did a breakthrough thing.
The achievement of a breakthrough was made by the scientists.
The scientists had a very big success, and they broke through.

questions.explanation: Nominalisation converts the verb achieve into the noun achievement, and the sentence becomes more compact and formal: The scientists' achievement of a breakthrough marked a turning point. (a) uses breakthrough as an adjective incorrectly. (b) is a correct passive nominalisation but wordy and awkward ("was made by" is a pseudo-passive). (d) is informal paraphrase, not nominalisation.

questions.prompt: Usage — Discourse cohesion: "Choose the option in which all discourse markers are used correctly."

options.text: The study was small; moreover, its findings were surprising.
She trained hard. However she won the race.
The plan failed. Therefore, it had been well-funded.
Although the results were poor but the team didn't give up.

questions.explanation: (a) is correct: moreover links a small size to surprising findings as two related noteworthy points (the smallness makes the findings more surprising — a compound idea). (b) needs a comma after However: However, she won. (c) makes a logical error: Therefore signals a result, but the plan failing and being well-funded are not in a cause-result relationship — Nevertheless would fit. (d) doubles the concession word: Although and but cannot both appear in the same clause — remove but (Although the results were poor, the team didn't give up).
